speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 25, 1996. I'm interviewing Miriam Buks. Her maiden name is Lutomirska. My name is Deborah Dobrusin. The interview is taking place in Skokie, Illinois, USA. And the language of the interview is English. My name is Deborah Dobrusin. Today is February 25, 1996. I'm interviewing Miriam Buks in Skokie, Illinois, USA. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam Buks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-U-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lutomirska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-T-O-M-I-R-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lubien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-B-I-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is March the 24th, 1904.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I am now? I will be 92 in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice. Lubien, which country is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland. And how many years did you live in Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, until I grew up. Well, I don't remember it exactly. I left about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was 18 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: About. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of town was Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of town? A very small town, a country town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your family do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] used to work by the summer orchards, and we picked the fruit and we brought it to town and sold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sold it in Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Youngest. And what was life like for you in Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like for a child. We had good parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have a school. My school time was no school. It's whatever I learned, I learned in private. Not much, but whatever I know, I know it from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a private tutor came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he teach all of your brothers and sisters together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Just one sister and me. The brothers were grown up already. They were much older than we girls were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: As they-- they learned just in the cheder when they were small children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you learn from the tutor?

speaker: interviewee
text: From what?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the tutor.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the tutor? We learned-- well, then it was Russia-- and Russian, Polish, Jewish, and you know, from the prayer books. That's Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know a lot of languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, languages I learned later. I went-- I learned in Poland, when I was a child, German, too. As [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I spoke German, Jewish, and Polish then. When I was in Italy, I learned Italian, I spoke. When I came to the States here, I was speaking Hebrew, Jewish, Polish, German, and Italian. Now I forgot the Hebrew and the Italian language. [? Now ?] I still speak Polish and German and Jewish. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's what was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were going up in Lubien, it was a part of Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was occupied by Russia. You see, when I was a child, Poland-- Russia occupied parts of Poland. It was divided. Austria had a part from Poland, and Germany has a part of Poland. When after the war, World War first ended, the every Pole-- Pole took back the parts that belonged to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your life change much when it was occupied by Russia? Or when it belonged to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I know as a child. It was occupied from Russia. After the First World War, I was already a grownup girl, because when the war broke out, I was 10 years old. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when it ended, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was maybe 14. I think it took four years of war. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in your family fight in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers were in the army. My older brother us in the army when the war broke out, and the other brother was in Odessa, and he was taken to the army, too, during the war. But, me, I was 10 years old. Wasn't much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they fought in the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And sure, the Poles were not [INAUDIBLE] country there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brothers come back to Lubien after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother came-- was went back to Russia, because he was lived in Russia. The other brother came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left Lubien when you were 18?

speaker: interviewee
text: About.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went not far from Lubien in a big city. I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wloclawek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I took care of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: In people's homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: In people's homes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years did you live in Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I used to go home because it wasn't far with the train. It took an hour to go home by train. It's June, 1933. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That I lived in Wloclawek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you left Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old? In 1933 I was 29 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went there when you were 18? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So you lived there 11 years? You lived there for quite a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: For quite a while, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And most of that time you took care for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have your own apartment or did you live in people's homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with the people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a living. I had to make a living. I had to work. When you are a child, you are with your parents. When you grow up, you cannot be with your parents. You have to-- And Lubien was a very small town. There was no opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone who lived in Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a friend of mine moved to Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danzig is on the border from Poland and Germany, I think. It was called Freistaat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Free city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was it called a free city?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't know. And she had ordering to come, as I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how far was it from Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was far. That was quite far. It was the border between Poland and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Wloclawek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wloclawek was not far from-- from where I was born. It was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which part of Poland was it in? Near the Russian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was in between. It was in the middle. It was not-- it was between Germany and Poland and Austria. It was in the middle. I think so. I don't know. It's so many years, who can remember? Especially when I was a child. I was so young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved to Danzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get home much after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From there I didn't go home. I don't think so-- I was home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was writing letters, and I got letters, but I don't think so. I don't remember going home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister still living at home at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my father died when I was about 18, 19 years old. He passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you had left Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then my mother was with my sister, living. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your mother do to make a living after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, women didn't work. She got-- I used to send her a little bit money, and my sister. And we helped her out. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sister stayed in Lubien with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do in Lubien?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did nothing. He did-- I don't remember what he did, her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she got married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years did you live in Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '33 till '39. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that happened before. What happened in '39, the war broke out. The Second World War broke out. Yeah, the Nazis were already there anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Danzig. It was Germany, part-- part Poland. But it was part Germany. Most of them were German people, they are living. Or lots of Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they treat the Jews in Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before, in beginning when I came, it was OK. But later it started with the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they control the government in Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they overpower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did everything that they wanted to do. We always were in fear, because you never knew when they would break in and arrest you, take you away to the concentration camps. But the living was OK, just we lived in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work for non-Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I worked for Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in their homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So were there restrictions placed on Jews during those six years that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For what? For restrictions for Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember. There probably was. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then there was rich Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they ran away from Poland. They moved to Danzig. It was an opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: An opportunity to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work, to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of Polish Jews went to Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Polish from Galicia, from everywhere. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And people, were they concerned that it was so close to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure they were concerned. But was no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no other way to make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no other way to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Danzig a large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was a beautiful city. Very Beautiful near the ocean, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the Baltic, the Baltic Ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was like Poland. It was at the border of Poland. At least with the train a half an hour, we were in Gdynia, Poland. A big [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] port. You know what I mean, port? A big port, Gdynia and Danzig. And so people would come to Danzig from the Baltic? From other countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: From other countries. From Poland, from Galicia. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the war, it was 1938, or about. It was-- it got very bad with the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the England-- Jew-- English Jews from England sent papers to lots of people. You have to know how-- have somebody to give your name, and I had a girlfriend, we lived together in the same house. Room. We shared a room. That was already from when the Nazis were there. I couldn't work. I didn't work. And she went to England, and she found a family for me, and they sent me papers to come to England. And I made the visa to, I made the passport to finish everything. The day I wanted-- I had to go to the train, the war broke out and I couldn't go anymore. I was stuck in Danzig. I couldn't-- couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] You must have been pretty upset.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it was. It was very-- we couldn't do nothing. We just had to depend on faith. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: To go back to Poland wasn't either so-- so good. What was happening in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, the German occupied Poland soon after the war started. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't do nothing. So in Danzig they stopped you from working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- oh, I got this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You know, it's so many years. I cannot remember. Many very important things I would like to remember myself, but I cannot remember it. I know there was committees from Jewish-- in the synagogue was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] help for the people, for the Jews. And every day the people used to go to the synagogue there. I forgot how they called it. They tried to find places to immigrate. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I-- really it's out of mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when England was close to you, did you go to the synagogue to get them to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. I just know we were like in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] surrounded with water, but we just couldn't help ourselves, couldn't do anything. Couldn't go out, couldn't go anywhere. We just depend on faith. Whatever will happen, will happen. And during the day it wasn't so bad. You could go out. But at night, we were afraid to go out. Even you were afraid to be in the house, because most of the time the Nazis broken in the houses at night and took out the Jews. And mostly they took out men members. It was very-- very dangerous for men. From one house that I know, they took out the son and the father, the oldest son and the father at night. And they never came back. As, you know, women were not so afraid like men. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Because I was going around with the English visa, and I couldn't leave. So one day I was walking in the street. I always had my passport with me, because we were afraid. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- I really don't know what makes me think to go to the Italian embassy, ask for a visa. I-- I don't remember why I did it. I just went-- went into the consulate, they opened the door, an old man-- a very good looking, you know, a fine looking man with a goatee-- came out and asked me what I want. I said-- I showed him the passport, and I said I would like to go to Italy. I cannot go to England. Maybe from Italy I may be able to go to England. He took the passport from me and he went into the other room and came out with a visa for six months. Well, I went home and then right away I went to the synagogue there, to the-- the organization. When I told them I had a visa, to Italy, everybody went-- made their way to go to the window. Everybody was on the-- envied me that I was so lucky. And I don't know they helped me. What they-- what was the help, what, I don't remember, but I have to make another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know I had to go to the city hall, which was Nazis. Wherever you go, you were afraid. Everything went good, went straight, because  I left, I think it was the 29th of the December. I don't remember. The 29th of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. And they came to the bahnhof. Bahnhof is the station, train station. I went in the-- how do you call it? The wagons. You call it different. There were four German soldiers. I was a lone woman, and I rode with them to the border of Italy. I was scared then. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember how I came in Trieste. It was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] an organization. They called it the Palestine Organization. And all the immigrants-- there were a thousand immigrants from Germany, from Austria, from Poland, from Hungary. It was-- they all-- every day they came there to the organization, and they helped them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They helped them with food. They helped them with money. They were very good. Until May, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19-- is it interesting?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] May, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a Jewish man from Romania. He arranged an illegal passage to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INTERPOSING VOICES] You got into Italy in 1939 in December?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you approached an organization, and they began helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They helped everybody, whoever came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: With where to live, and where to eat, everything. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
